entry.title: Unlocking Document Intelligence: AI-Powered Summarization in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's fast-paced business environment, time is precious. Document review shouldn't consume hours of your workday. That's why we're thrilled to highlight SAP Cloud ALM's free or charge AI-powered document summarization feature—a game-changing capability that leverages artificial intelligence to instantly transform lengthy documents into concise, actionable summaries.

Whether you're managing requirements, implementation guides, or technical specifications, AI-generated summaries provide immediate value by giving you the essential information at a glance.

Example:_________________________________________________________________________

________________________________________________________________________________

What is AI-Powered Document Summarization?

Intelligent Content Analysis

SAP Cloud ALM uses advanced AI capabilities to automatically analyze and summarize document content. Rather than forcing you to read through pages of text, the system intelligently extracts key points and presents them in a clear, digestible format.

Immediate Overview, Maximum Efficiency

The AI-generated summary appears directly above your document content, providing:

  • Instant context: Understand the document's purpose without reading everything
  • Quick decision-making: Identify relevant information immediately
  • Reduced cognitive load: Let AI handle the heavy lifting of content extraction
  • Time savings: Spend minutes, not hours, understanding document content

What the AI Considers

The summarization engine analyzes multiple dimensions of your document:

Element

Purpose

Title

Provides context and document purpose

Content

The primary material being summarized

Document Type

Tailors summary format to document category

What is the Structure of the summary

The summary is usually generated in four sections extracted from the document content:

  • Purpose
  • Key points
  • Critical details
  • Conclusions

Prerequisites, Availability and Creation Procedure

Please follow the online documentation provided under the link in chapter Summarizing Documents to find more information about regional availability, prerequisites and the step-by-step usage procedure.

Security and Content Filtering

Protecting Sensitive Information

Your security is paramount. SAP Cloud ALM employs content filtering during the summarization process:

  • Continuous monitoring: Content filtering checks document information while the AI creates your summary
  • Sensitive data protection: The system helps protect confidential and sensitive information
  • Security-first approach: If the content filtering system detects potentially sensitive material, the summarization process fails gracefully
  • Compliance assurance: This ensures your organization maintains data protection standards

What to do if summarization fails: If content filtering triggers and summarization fails, review your document for potentially sensitive information that may need redaction or removal. Contact your organization's security team if you need guidance.

Best Practices for Optimal Results

Ensure Quality Content

  • Use clear, well-structured document content
  • AI performs best when source material is organized and explicit
  • Remove unnecessary fluff or redundant information

Use Descriptive Titles

  • Include meaningful titles that reflect document purpose
  • The AI uses titles to provide context for summarization

Classify Documents Appropriately

  • Assign the correct Document Type
  • This helps the AI tailor the summary format to your content

Review Before Applying

  • Always review AI-generated summaries
  • Make manual adjustments if needed
  • Use the Regenerate function if the summary misses key points

Leverage for Different Document Types

  • Use summaries for requirements documents to highlight critical requirements
  • Apply to specifications to capture key technical details
  • Use for implementation guides to identify crucial steps

Conclusion

AI-powered document summarization in SAP Cloud ALM is transforming how teams interact with documentation. By providing instant, intelligent summaries with security-first design, the feature empowers organizations to:

  • Work faster
  • Make better decisions
  • Reduce information overload
  • Maintain security standards

Start leveraging AI-generated summaries today and experience the difference intelligent document management can make for your organization.

entry.title: Depreciation Recalculation Issue After Useful Life Correction – NBV Reset & Incorrect Future Values
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am facing an issue with asset depreciation recalculation after correcting the useful life post-migration, and I would appreciate your guidance.

Scenario

During migration, the remaining useful life for certain assets was incorrectly maintained. As a result, depreciation posted from January onwards does not reflect correct values, causing variances in asset balances.

To correct this, the following approach was taken:

  • Calculated the difference between correct and incorrectly posted depreciation
  • Posted a manual depreciation adjustment 
  • Updated the remaining useful life to the correct value

This approach was tested on two assets

Expected Behaviour

After performing the above steps, the system should:

  • Recalculate monthly depreciation correctly based on the revised useful life
  • Maintain the correct Net Book Value (NBV)
  • Ensure future depreciation runs reflect accurate values

Actual Behaviour

Asset 3123 – Issue Observed

  • Capitalisation Value: 4635.88
  • Correct Useful Life: 13 months → Expected Monthly Depreciation: 356.60
  • Incorrect Useful Life: 36 months → Depreciation: 128.74

Actions performed:

  • Manual depreciation posted for Jan–July variance
  • Useful life updated from 36 → 13

Issues:

  • NBV becomes zero immediately
  • Ordinary depreciation equals full capitalisation value (4635.88)
  • Monthly depreciation recalculated as 253.40 (incorrect) instead of 356.60
  • In August depreciation run, system shows 1126.02 (unexpected)

Asset 3030 – Issue in Depreciation Run

  • Capitalisation Value: 5772.80
  • Correct Useful Life: 64 months → Expected Monthly Depreciation: 90.20
  • Incorrect Useful Life: 77 months → Depreciation: 74.98

Actions performed:

  • Manual depreciation adjustment posted
  • Useful life updated from 77 → 64

Result:

  • Monthly depreciation is correctly recalculated at 90.20 in master data
  • However, depreciation run still shows incorrect values

Observation

  • Both assets use the same depreciation type
  • The same correction approach was applied
  • However, the system behaviour is inconsistent

Questions

  1. Why is the system recalculating incorrect depreciation values after updating the useful life?
  2. Why is NBV being set to zero in Asset 3123?
  3. Why is the depreciation run not reflecting corrected values (especially for Asset 3030)?
  4. Is there any additional step required after posting manual depreciation and updating useful life?

Any suggestions or guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: SAP - SD Output type triggering
entry.content:
In standard SAP output determination, if you want to exclude an output type for certain customers without using a Requirement routine, the usual way is to adjust the access sequence and condition tables so that the customer dimension is explicitly part of the determination.

Right now, table 901 (Sales Org/Dist.Chl/Div/Bill type) is too broad. It triggers outputs regardless of customer. To prevent emails for specific customers:

Option 1: Use condition table 006 (includes Customer) in the access sequence, so determination checks customer before assigning the output type.

Option 2: Adjust the access sequence priority so that customer‑specific tables (like 006 or 907) are checked first, and 901 only acts as fallback.

Option 3: If you must keep 901, then maintain condition records carefully — exclude those customers by not creating records for them in 901, and rely on 006/907 for controlled determination.

This way, you avoid adding a Requirement routine, but still control output determination through condition tables and access sequence logic.

entry.title: Re: Data analyzer dimension/measure show/hide
entry.content:
Hello @Shashank_Vyas 


Use Hidden property (if column truly not needed anywhere)

Set the field as Hidden in the Analytic Model → it disappears everywhere (including Story).

 

Create a Consumption/Projection View

Build a separate view/model:

Model A → full data (for Story)
Model B → exclude unwanted columns (for Data Analyzer)


Use Model B for Data Analyzer.


Create two analytical models:

One for Story (full fields)
One for Data Analyzer (restricted fields)

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Powering SAP Cloud Transformation with Advanced Data Migration and Continuous Testing
entry.content:
@RACHID_AITBOUALE  

My apologies for the delay.  

Essentially, SAP Cloud ALM provides open APIs which could be used, and with that Cloud ALM remains as the orchestration and documentation hub. Otherwise, the automated testing is running in parallel without any traceability to requirements and processes. 

When using the Tricentis integrated toolchain, I’d focus less on the AI tooling itself and more on the integration seams. Treat SAP S/4HANA as the system of record, and test the APIs, events, and data contracts it exposes using Tricentis Tosca’s API and integration testing capabilities.  The key is contract‑first testing and isolating AI behavior variability, so your core SAP processes remain stable and testable.

At a high level,  Syniti uses ML to provide context‑aware recommendations for non‑standard data—learning from past patterns and steward decisions—so operationally critical port assets aren’t flagged as errors just because they don’t fit standard models.

entry.title: IFRS 16 implementation via Scope Item 1T6 in a single-ledger setup — impacts and best practices
entry.content:
Dear community,

I'm working on an S/4HANA Public Cloud implementation for a logistics group (multiple companies: warehousing, transportation, port terminals, inspection services) and would like to validate our financial reporting architecture with the community.

Architecture overview:

  • Leading Ledger 0L — official IFRS reporting basis, covering both IFRS 15 (Revenue) and IFRS 16 (Leases)
  • Extension Ledger — management view only, excluding IFRS 16 effects so that lease contracts are reflected as direct rental expense (the way the board prefers to read the P&L). It does not serve any statutory standard.

Scope items under evaluation:

  • 1T6 — Lease-In Accounting (confirmed): to handle IFRS 16 in 0L
  • 3KK — Revenue Accounting and Reporting (RAR): under evaluation against standard J58 for IFRS 15
  • J58 — Accounting and Financial Close (baseline): already in scope

The IFRS 15 question — J58 or 3KK?

The customer's business is mostly event-based revenue recognition (services rendered = revenue recognized), which J58 would cover well. However, we've identified scenarios that may push us toward 3KK:

  • Take-or-pay contracts in port and warehousing operations (reserved capacity + variable usage), which seem to imply multiple performance obligations within a single contract
  • Deferred revenue scenarios where contract signature/invoicing date differs from the period in which the service is delivered
  • Potential bundling of services (storage + handling + inspection) sold as a package with a single price

I'd appreciate the community's input on the following points:

  1. IFRS 15 — when does 3KK become essential vs. J58? For those who implemented logistics or capacity-based businesses with take-or-pay clauses, did J58 standard hold up, or did you need to bring in 3KK / RAR? At what point did the multi-PO allocation, contract modifications, or breakage treatment force the move to RAR?
  2. 1T6 — Chart of Accounts impact: beyond the standard accounts that come with the reference CoA, what additional GL accounts have you typically needed to create for ROU Assets, Lease Liabilities (current/non-current split), depreciation of ROU, and interest expense on lease liabilities? Any account determination pitfalls worth flagging?
  3. Extension ledger for management view: for those who used an extension ledger to deliver a "pre-IFRS 16" management view, what was your approach — automated reversing entries, manual postings, or a reporting-only solution? How did you keep the extension ledger reconciled over time as new lease contracts are added or modified?
  4. Transition of legacy contracts (IFRS 16): full retrospective vs. modified retrospective — what worked best in your projects? Any lessons learned on initial measurement of ROU and liability?
  5. Apps and process: which Fiori apps from 1T6 (and from 3KK, for those who activated it) have you found most useful in day-to-day operation — contract creation, valuation runs, posting runs, reconciliation, reporting?
  6. Scope decisions: how have you handled IFRS 16 short-term lease and low-value asset exemptions in practice within the Public Cloud setup?

Any references to SAP Best Practices documentation, blog posts, or real-project experiences would be very welcome.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: Geting the user hardware key using the the di api or...
entry.content:
Hello,

 

Yes, you can get it programatically. First go to SLD, and find out where is located your license manager:

 

So, we have for this example the address for the SLD:

https://myserver:40000/ControlCenter/

And for the LicenseControlCenter:

https://myserver:40002/LicenseControlCenter/

Then, you can use this URL to get the hardware key:


https://myserver:40002/license/GetHardwareKey

 

And this is the output:

 

Other useful URLs:

https://myserver:40002/license/GetLicenseInfo
https://myserver:40002/license/GetLicenseStatus
https://myserver:40002/license/GetUserCount
https://myserver:40002/license/CheckLicense

Best Regards,

William Wachholz - https://williamwachholz.vercel.app/

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1: Why the Change Record Matters More Than Ever
entry.content:
With SAP S/4HANA 2025 Feature Package Stack 01 (FPS1), SAP continues its “innovation without disruption” strategy—but one enhancement stands out for engineering and manufacturing organizations: the strengthened positioning of Change Records in Engineering Change Management (ECM).

While Change Records already existed before, FPS1 reinforces their role as the central orchestration object for planned, cross‑functional changes, especially where BOMs, routings, documents, and materials must be changed in a coordinated way. Rather than executing isolated updates, Change Records provide a structured process that answers why a change exists, who owns it, and where it stands in the approval and execution lifecycle.

What’s New in 2025 FPS1?

SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1 does not introduce radical redesigns, but it sharpens the process clarity and usability of Change Records:

  • Clear separation of roles
    Change Records control the process and governance, while change numbers handle the technical execution of object changes.

  • Improved lifecycle control
    Statuses such as Initial, Start Change Process, Approval, and Completed are emphasized as the primary steering mechanism across departments.

  • Better planning relevance
    Engineering effectivity dates and expected completion dates are positioned as planning tools for logistics and manufacturing—not just documentation fields.

  • Low adoption risk
    As with other FPS releases, these improvements are incremental and compatible, making Change Records easier to adopt without redesigning existing ECM setups.

Why This Matters for Manufacturing and Engineering Teams

For engineering, production, and supply‑chain teams, Change Records offer tangible benefits:

  • One change, many objects
    A single Change Record can govern BOMs, routings, documents, and materials—while keeping consistency across them.

  • Built‑in governance
    Approval workflows and processing statuses reduce reliance on emails, spreadsheets, and external change logs.

  • Improved auditability
    Change intent, execution, and completion are fully traceable—an increasingly important requirement for regulated industries.

  • Complement, not replace
    SAP is very clear: Change Records do not eliminate change numbers. Instead, they provide the process framework around them.

This dual model—Change Records for orchestration, change numbers for execution—becomes clearer and more robust with SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1.

 

How to Explore the Details

All official details are documented in the SAP Help Portal. To explore Change Record related enhancements and the full FPS1 scope, see:

  • What’s New in SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1 
To explore Change Record in general, see:
  • Change Record 
To deepen your understanding of Change Records in SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1, SAP provides a rich set of videos:
  • Video Library for Change Management

entry.title: Re: 1E3: Custom Ordertyp for Kanban with external Procure...
entry.content:
Dear @ZBMA,

I do not see a released in-app extension or Badi that allows us to override the PO document type / number range used by standard Kanban external procurement. SAP provides Kanban extensibility but the relevant Badi Change Before Creation of Replenishment Element is described for changing custom field values before the replenishment element is created, not for replacing the purchasing document type

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/4c7b54ad7f2d41ae99be5370fed66c0b.html

A side-by-side custom process on SAP BTP could theoretically create POs with a dedicated document type via released APIs but that would replace/extend the standard Kanban replenishment logic and would need separate design for status handling, errors, goods receipt, and process ownership. I would not position this as a simple extension workaround

So there is no clean standard Public Edition extension to meet the requirement 1:1. The recommended next step is an SAP incident to confirm the read-only behavior and if confirmed by SAP an enhancement request / SAP Influence item

More details below:

https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/s4hana-cloud/continuous-influence

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: How to access custom VBAK fields in BAPI_SALESORDER_C...
entry.content: Yes, it is possible, but only if you enhance BAPE_VBAK and handle the mapping via the BAPI extension interface. The fields created in the Custom Fields app won’t show up automatically and you need to wire them into the BAPI manually.
